title: Global Mobility Has Slowed, but the Race for AI Talent Has Not
gist: BCG's Top Talent Tracker shows global highly-skilled migration fell 11.6% in 2025 (to 3.3M moves), with AI talent down 12%, yet the US gained share in highly-skilled, STEM, and research talent—while ceding ground specifically in AI talent, the one category where its lead stopped widening. Gulf states (UAE, Saudi Arabia) and India are emerging as new magnets.

insights.claim: Cross-border moves by highly skilled professionals fell from 3.7 million in 2024 to 3.3 million in 2025, an 11.6% drop equal to roughly 430,000 fewer movers, accelerating a slowdown that began in 2024.
insights.speaker: Johann Harnoss
insights.so_what: A structural cooling in skilled migration tightens labor supply for innovation-dependent firms and raises wage pressure in scarce-skill pools.
insights.quote: Cross-border moves by highly skilled professionals fell from 3.7 million in 2024 to 3.3 million last year—a drop of 11.6%.

insights.claim: The 2025 mobility decline was sharpest for specialist talent: STEM –13%, AI –12%, and research (PhD) talent –19%.
insights.speaker: Johann Harnoss
insights.so_what: The scarcest, highest-value cohorts are moving least, intensifying the localization of advanced capability and frontier-AI hiring competition.
insights.quote: The drop was sharper for specialist talent: STEM at –13%, AI at –12%, and research at –19%.

themes.theme: US position and the AI exception

insights.claim: Through end-2025 the US gained share in three of four talent categories—adding 4.0pp in highly skilled, 2.6pp in STEM, and 1.2pp in research talent—but AI talent was the sole category where the US ceded ground.
insights.speaker: Johann Harnoss
insights.so_what: The first crack in US AI-talent dominance is a leading indicator for where frontier-AI capability and capital may diffuse next decade.
insights.quote: AI talent is the exception: it's the one talent category where the US ceded ground, signaling a more contested race for AI talent globally.

insights.claim: BCG frames whether the US's stalled AI-talent lead is a temporary leveling or a structural shift as potentially one of the defining questions of the next decade of the global technology race.
insights.speaker: Johann Harnoss
insights.so_what: Elevates AI-talent share to a macro-strategic signal worth monitoring alongside chips and compute for US tech leadership.
insights.quote: Whether that proves a temporary leveling or the start of a structural shift may become one of the defining questions of the next decade of the global technology race.

insights.claim: BCG attributes continued US talent attraction to pull factors—compensation, opportunity, and ecosystem depth—outweighing tighter, more selective immigration policy under the second Trump administration.
insights.speaker: Johann Harnoss
insights.so_what: Suggests policy headwinds have not yet broken the US talent magnet, but the margin is narrowing—watch for an inflection.
insights.quote: This suggests that pull factors (compensation, opportunity, ecosystem depth) still outweigh policy headwinds for highly skilled workers.

insights.claim: The UAE drew nearly 194,000 highly skilled workers (adding 0.8pp share) and Saudi Arabia posted the highest retention ratio (2.6x) of any major destination, with Hong Kong, Sweden, and Japan also gaining inflows.
insights.speaker: Johann Harnoss
insights.so_what: Gulf states are converting capital into talent share—supportive for their long-run diversification and tech-sector buildouts.
insights.quote: the UAE, which drew nearly 194,000 highly skilled workers, thus adding 0.8 points of market share, and Saudi Arabia, which recorded the highest retention ratio (2.6x) of any major destination.

insights.claim: Canada, the UK, and European countries saw the sharpest drops in talent inflows—Canada fell from a top-three to seventh place in highly skilled talent, while the UAE is rapidly closing on the UK in highly skilled and AI categories.
insights.speaker: Johann Harnoss
insights.so_what: Relative decline in Anglo/European talent capture is a headwind for their domestic innovation ecosystems and a tailwind for Gulf rivals.
insights.quote: In highly skilled talent, Canada dropped from a top-three target to seventh place.

insights.claim: India ranks among the top-three destinations for STEM and AI talent, but the largest source countries (US, UK, UAE, Canada) suggest this is mainly nonresident Indians returning home rather than broad foreign attraction.
insights.speaker: Johann Harnoss
insights.so_what: India's talent inflow is a reverse-brain-drain story—meaningful for its tech sector but not yet a sign of global talent magnetism.
insights.quote: suggesting this is largely nonresident Indians (NRIs) returning home rather than broad-based attraction of new foreign talent.
